intro: May 2012. You just IPO'd Facebook — and the stock is speedrunning downward, $38 toward $18. The problem is existential: a billion people are moving to phones, and Facebook makes basically zero money on mobile. You run Facebook, and the market is convinced you fumbled the biggest shift of the decade.

realOutcome.summary: Facebook got written off as a desktop dinosaur and became a mobile-ad juggernaut. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: Your money comes from desktop ads, but your users are dipping to phones where you barely monetize. Reorient the ENTIRE company around mobile — brutal — or defend the desktop cash cow?

choices.reveal: Zuckerberg forced the whole company to reorganize around mobile, yanking engineers off desktop features. Mobile ads went from nearly nothing to the overwhelming majority of revenue. Hard reset, correct call.

choices.text: Defend the desktop advertising cash cow
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You protect today's money while your users migrate to a screen you can't monetize. Facebook slowly becomes 'that site people used to check on the family computer.'

choices.text: Rush a Facebook-branded phone to market
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You try to own the hardware and ship a phone nobody wants. (Facebook actually tried this with 'Facebook Home' — instant flop.) The answer was software, not a phone. Fumbled the read.

decisions.scenario: A photo app called Instagram just went nuclear — 13 employees, zero revenue. You can buy it for ~$1 billion, a price the press will call absolutely unhinged for a company that makes no money.

choices.reveal: Facebook bought Instagram for ~$1B in 2012, roasted at the time as a wild overpay. It became one of the greatest acquisitions in history and a pillar worth many times the price. Steal of the century.

choices.text: Build a photo app in-house instead
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You try to clone Instagram instead of buying it. It keeps growing solo, a rival snaps it up, and it becomes the competitor that eats all your young users. Cloned yourself out of a win.

choices.text: Ignore Instagram as a passing fad
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You dismiss a literal rocket ship. Instagram becomes where the entire next generation lives, and you own zero percent of it. Historic fumble.

decisions.scenario: You need to make money on mobile without nuking the experience. Put ads directly into the mobile News Feed — risky but native — or keep ads in the safe old desktop sidebar?

choices.text: Put ads natively in the mobile News Feed
choices.reveal: Facebook dropped ads straight into the mobile feed, a format that felt native and performed absurdly well. It solved the mobile-money problem and became one of the most effective ad products ever built. Printer goes brrr.

choices.text: Keep ads in the desktop sidebar only
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You keep ads off mobile to 'protect the experience' and keep making no money where all your users are. Beautiful app, no business model. Broke and beloved.

choices.text: Build the 'Facebook Home' phone skin instead
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You chase owning the phone's home screen instead of nailing feed ads. (Facebook Home really shipped in 2013 and died fast.) You solved the wrong problem with confidence.

decisions.scenario: A messaging app called WhatsApp has half a BILLION users and is spreading worldwide. It could become a rival platform. Buying it would cost a staggering $19 billion.

choices.text: Buy WhatsApp for $19B to own messaging
choices.reveal: Facebook bought WhatsApp for $19B in 2014 — a jaw-dropping number. It locked up global messaging, kept a rival out of enemy hands, and grew past two billion users. Expensive, correct.

choices.text: Build your own messenger and out-compete them
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You try to beat WhatsApp instead of buying it. It stays independent, a rival grabs it, and you've handed a competitor a direct line to billions of phones. Big oops.

choices.text: Let a rival buy WhatsApp, it's too pricey
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Google or another giant scoops up WhatsApp. Global messaging — and billions of users' relationships — now belongs to your enemy. Penny wise, planet foolish.

decisions.scenario: You own Instagram AND WhatsApp. Some execs want to cram them into the main Facebook app right now to juice the core numbers. Others say let them run free.

choices.text: Let the acquired apps run independently
choices.reveal: Facebook let Instagram and WhatsApp keep their own teams, cultures, and products for years. Autonomy let them grow to billions each instead of getting smothered by the mothership. Let them cook.

choices.text: Fold them into the main Facebook app now
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You cram your new apps into Facebook to farm engagement and break what made them special. Forced integration kills the golden geese you just paid billions for.

choices.text: Shut them down to push everyone to Facebook
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You kill your own acquisitions to protect the flagship. You spent billions to delete your rivals and then deleted the value with them. Speedrun any% self-sabotage.

bands.line: You turned the mobile threat into a mobile empire. Legend episode: WON. No notes.
A solid social company — not the platform that owns your phone. Mid.
A desktop dinosaur the phones left behind. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Facebook 20x'd after this fork. Today's agents flagged a company told it missed the next platform →
